objections.description: The plan rejects react-window to preserve Ctrl+F but then proposes chunked DOM append via IntersectionObserver. For documents exceeding 50,000 words this still means the full DOM eventually materializes — the 5,000 DOM node budget in §9 is mathematically incompatible with rendering a 50k-word legal document that has nested structure, mark highlights, and anchor IDs. A single 50k-word document with article/paragraph/inciso nesting easily produces 8,000–15,000 nodes. There is no stated fallback or degradation strategy when the budget is exceeded, and the dev-mode alert is not a solution — it's a notification of failure. This is the core reading experience; shipping without a concrete reconciliation between 'contiguous DOM for Ctrl+F' and 'DOM budget of 5,000' will result in either broken search or broken performance on mid-range Android.
objections.suggested_fix: Define explicit tiers: (1) <5k words: full render. (2) 5k–20k words: chunked append, accept DOM budget overshoot up to 8k nodes, log but don't alert. (3) >20k words: render visible sections ±2, but ALSO implement a custom in-document search bar (Cmd+F equivalent) that searches the raw text/JSON model rather than relying on browser Ctrl+F. This lets you unmount off-screen sections without losing searchability. State clearly that browser Ctrl+F is degraded for tier 3 documents and the in-app search replaces it. Add the in-app document search component to the file list and timebox.

objections.location: Section 5a: Legal Hierarchy Rendering — flat HTML fallback
objections.description: The plan says 'Parse server-side or client-side using regex/DOM parsing for Art., §, Roman numeral incisos, letter alíneas' and then 'Degrade gracefully: if parsing fails, render as plain formatted text.' This is dangerously hand-wavy for the feature that defines the entire product. DOU documents have inconsistent formatting across decades of publications: some use 'Art.' some use 'ART.', paragraph markers vary (§, Parágrafo único), roman numerals can be uppercase or lowercase, and editorial notes/retificações break expected patterns. A regex-based parser with no test corpus and no stated coverage target will silently mis-parse documents, producing incorrect legal hierarchy — which for a government document product is worse than showing plain text. There is no mention of a parsing test suite or accuracy measurement.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a concrete deliverable: before building the parser, pull 20 diverse real documents via gabi-es (varying organs, decades, sections) and create a `tests/fixtures/dou-structure/` directory. Write the parser with these as test cases. Define an acceptance threshold: parser must correctly identify article boundaries in ≥90% of test fixtures. Documents that fail parsing get plain-text rendering with a visible 'structured view unavailable' indicator, not silent degradation. Add 2 days to the viewer timebox for parser development and test fixture creation.

objections.description: The plan proposes runtime regex parsing of cross-references like 'Art. 5° da Lei 8.666/93' and then a live lookup via gabi-es to check corpus existence. This is an N+1 query pattern executed during document render. A 50-article regulatory document can easily contain 30–100 cross-references. Firing 30–100 Elasticsearch lookups on document open will blow the 2.0s FCP budget from §9 and create unpredictable load on the backend. There is no caching strategy, no batching strategy, and no stated limit on concurrent lookups.
objections.suggested_fix: Cross-reference resolution must be either: (A) precomputed at index time and stored as a field on the document (preferred — add to 'Requires backend work' column in §0), or (B) done as a single batch query after render (extract all reference patterns, fire one multi-search request, then decorate links asynchronously). Option B is acceptable for MVP but must not block initial render. Render all references as styled non-interactive text first, then upgrade to links as batch results return. Add this to the architecture and adjust the timebox.

objections.location: Section 4: Bottom Sheet Primitives — gesture conflicts
objections.description: The plan defines 'Drag on handle zone (top 48px) controls sheet position. Below handle, internal content scrolls normally' but does not address the well-known iOS Safari and Chrome mobile issue where overscroll-behavior on nested scrollable containers inside a sheet causes the sheet to move when the inner content reaches scroll boundaries. This is the single most common source of jank in bottom sheet implementations on mobile web and the 3-day timebox doesn't account for the browser-specific debugging this requires.
objections.suggested_fix: Explicitly state the overscroll containment strategy: use `overscroll-behavior: contain` on inner scroll containers, use touch-action manipulation on the handle zone, and test specifically on iOS Safari 16+ and Chrome Android 120+ where behavior differs. If the custom implementation proves fragile within 2 days, have a named fallback library (e.g., vaul by emilkowalski) that handles these edge cases. Add browser-specific testing to the done-when criteria.

objections.description: The plan specifies @tanstack/react-virtual for result sets over 50 items but doesn't address that virtualized lists break browser Ctrl+F (the same issue acknowledged for the document viewer). For search results this is a real usability problem — users searching legal documents frequently use Ctrl+F to scan result snippets for specific terms. Additionally, the plan doesn't specify what happens with URL-synced pagination: are results paginated server-side or is the full result set fetched and virtualized client-side? This has massive implications for backend load and time-to-first-result.
objections.suggested_fix: Clarify the pagination model: server-side pagination with page/cursor in URL params (preferred for a search product). If server-side paginated with 20–50 results per page, virtualization is unnecessary — just render the page. Reserve @tanstack/react-virtual only if you confirm the backend returns unbounded result sets with no pagination support. Add pagination model to the §0 backend audit questions.

objections.description: The reading position schema stores `scrollPercent` but the viewer uses section-based anchors for TOC and deep linking. Scroll percentage is fragile: it changes meaning if the document's rendered height changes (font size change, collapsed sections expanding, viewport resize). The resume prompt says 'Resume from Art. 15?' which implies section-based positioning, but the schema only has `lastSectionId` as nullable — suggesting scroll percent is the primary mechanism.
objections.suggested_fix: Make `lastSectionId` the primary resume mechanism, non-nullable for structured documents. Use `scrollPercent` only as a fallback for documents where section parsing failed. Update the schema to: `{ documentId: string, lastSectionId: string, scrollOffsetWithinSection: number, scrollPercentFallback: number, timestamp: number }`. This survives font size changes and collapsed section state changes.

objections.description: LCP < 1.5s on 4G throttle is aggressive for a search-and-read app that must fetch results from Elasticsearch. The plan doesn't distinguish between LCP on the home/empty state (achievable — it's mostly static) and LCP on a search results page or document viewer (which depends entirely on backend response time). A single Elasticsearch query on a large corpus can take 200–800ms, and 4G throttle adds ~150ms RTT. Combined with JS parse/eval on mid-range Android, 1.5s is likely not achievable for the search results route without server-side rendering or aggressive prefetching, neither of which is mentioned.
objections.suggested_fix: Split the LCP target by route: Home/empty state: <1.5s. Search results: <2.5s (with skeleton UI shown within 500ms). Document viewer: <2.0s for first section content (the rest loads progressively). Add skeleton/loading state components to the deliverable list — they are not mentioned anywhere in the plan but are critical for perceived performance.

objections.description: The plan removes the analytics tab and settings tab, leaving only Search and Saved. But the plan also introduces typography controls, reading mode toggle, and font controls — all of which are 'settings-like' features scattered across contextual locations. There's no discoverability story for these. A new user opening a long document has no way to know font size controls exist unless they tap through MobileActionsBar actions. For a professional reading tool, discoverability of reading comfort features is table stakes.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a brief onboarding tooltip or first-visit indicator on the MobileActionsBar's typography action (e.g., a subtle dot badge that clears on first use). This costs almost nothing to implement and solves the discoverability gap without adding a settings tab.

suggestions.location: Section 5: Document Viewer — missing offline/error states
suggestions.description: The viewer section has no mention of error handling: what happens when the document fetch fails (network error, 404, 500)? What about partial document loads (chunked rendering where one chunk fails to parse)? For a professional reading tool, these states need explicit design. Add: (1) Document fetch error → full-page error state with retry button, (2) Parse error for specific section → inline error placeholder within the document body ('Section could not be rendered'), (3) Network loss during reading → continue showing already-rendered content with an offline banner.

The empty state shows 'today's DOU publication count' and '5 most recent publications from each section.' This requires a dedicated backend endpoint or a date-filtered aggregation query that fires on every app open. If the user opens the app on a weekend or holiday when there are no publications, the empty state shows '0 new publications today' — which is a dead end. Design for the zero-publications case: fall back to 'most recent publications' without a date qualifier, and show the actual date of the latest publication.

The plan says 'dark-first' but never addresses light mode at all. Even if dark is the default, users reading dense legal text in daylight conditions will need a light mode. The design token architecture should include both themes from the start — retrofitting a light theme onto warm-gray dark tokens is painful because the semantic mapping (surface, elevation, emphasis levels) reverses. At minimum, define the light mode neutral scale alongside the dark scale in design-tokens.ts even if only the dark theme ships in MVP.

suggestions.location: Section 5g: Reading Mode — keyboard shortcut Alt+R
suggestions.description: Alt+R conflicts with browser-level shortcuts on some platforms (Alt activates the menu bar on Windows Firefox). Use a less collision-prone shortcut or make it configurable. Also, the plan mentions no keyboard shortcut reference/cheat sheet — for a tool with Cmd+K, Alt+R, Escape behaviors, and arrow key navigation, a discoverable shortcut reference (shown inside CommandPalette as a tab) would serve the power-user persona well.

The plan has no risk register or contingency planning. The biggest risk is §0 (backend audit) revealing that document bodies are unstructured raw text with no field-level filtering, which would invalidate §5a structured rendering, §5b TOC generation, §5d cross-references, and §6a filter UI. The plan should state: 'If §0 reveals flat text bodies with no field filtering, the viewer timebox increases by 3 days for parser development and the search timebox decreases by 2 days (fewer filter features). Total timeline impact: +1 day net.'

suggestions.location: Section 5: Document Viewer — text selection and copy
suggestions.description: For a legal document reader, text selection and copy-with-citation is a primary workflow. Users need to select a passage, copy it, and get the text plus a citation reference (e.g., 'Art. 5, § 3º — DOU Seção 1, 15/03/2024, p. 42'). The plan has no mention of text selection behavior, copy formatting, or citation generation. This is a differentiation opportunity that directly serves the professional user persona.
